one_line_takeaway: Individually benign MCP tools can be chained by an externally planted 'parasitic prompt' into no-server, no-interaction privacy-exfiltration attacks, and a census of 12,230 tools / 1,360 servers finds 8.7% of tools and 27.2% of servers already provide the needed gadgets.
key_finding: Exploitable 'gadgets' are pervasive, not rare: of 12,230 tools, capability analysis flagged 5,666 as potentially risky and dynamic verification confirmed 1,062 (8.7%) with at least one exploitable capability; of 1,360 servers, 370 (27.2%) expose at least one such tool. Complementary capabilities let attackers assemble full ingestion->collection->disclosure chains across servers, and 2 tools plus 2 servers alone cover all three phases (a self-contained attack). End-to-end, 9 of 10 constructed toolchains achieved verified exfiltration in at least one of 10 trials, with the strongest chains succeeding 6-8/10; the single failing chain (Chain 8) involved functionally unrelated tools, showing that functional relevance among tools drives success. The cross-model sweep shows success depends heavily on the underlying LLM's safety alignment rather than the client: GPT-5 and Claude-4.5-Sonnet resisted every attack (0/5 across all conditions), whereas DeepSeek-R1 and Gemini-2.5-Flash were compromised in nearly every case.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes a new class of stealthy privacy-leakage attacks on the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, which the authors call Parasitic Toolchain Attacks, instantiated as MCP Unintended Privacy Disclosure (MCP-UPD). Its distinguishing claim over prior MCP work is that the attack requires NO malicious server and NO direct victim interaction: instead of poisoning a tool the user installs, the adversary plants a 'parasitic prompt' in an external data source (a forum post, shared document, email, chat history) that the victim's legitimate toolchain later ingests. The attack then chains multiple individually-benign tools across (or within) different servers into a coordinated workflow that unfolds in three automated phases: (1) Parasitic Ingestion (an external-ingestion tool such as fetch/search pulls in the malicious instructions), (2) Privacy Collection (a privacy-access tool such as read_file/read_mail harvests local files or private data), and (3) Privacy Disclosure (a network-access tool such as send_mail/postToWeb exfiltrates the data to an attacker endpoint). The second concrete contribution is MCP-SEC, an automated three-stage measurement framework (Description Collector, LLM-based Tool Capability Analyzer with manual check, and a Dynamic Verifier that actually runs tools in a real Cursor host), used to conduct what the authors call the first large-scale security census of the MCP ecosystem. The root-cause argument is that MCP shifts the LLM from passive information processor to autonomous tool orchestrator without adapting security boundaries, producing two systemic flaws: lack of context-tool isolation and absence of least-privilege enforcement.
threat_model: Adversary goal: unauthorized exfiltration of data reachable by the victim's MCP Host (local config files with API keys, chat/email history, data from connected MCP services). Adversary capabilities are deliberately conservative: (1) an Instruction Injection Vector, i.e. the ability to place a crafted textual 'parasitic prompt' into an external data source the victim will later retrieve via a legitimate tool (malicious forum post, poisoned shared document, indexed web page); and (2) an Exfiltration Channel, i.e. control of a network endpoint (email inbox or web server) able to receive stolen data. Explicit non-capabilities / assumptions: the attacker CANNOT compromise the Host, the embedded LLM, or any MCP Server; cannot eavesdrop on or tamper with Host-Server network traffic; and needs no prior knowledge of the victim's local files or MCP configuration. The vulnerability is assumed to emerge purely from trusted interactions across components that are each secure in isolation. Attack impact is bounded by the privileges of the victim's Host, and end-to-end success is highest when the Host runs in auto-execution mode (the paper cites reported auto-approval rates of ~20% for new users rising past 40% with experience).
limitations: Author-acknowledged: about 30% of servers require platform tokens tied to external services that were hard to register for (credit-card or enterprise verification), so they could not be measured; other servers depend on heavy external environments (Kubernetes, MySQL, desktop apps) costly to configure at scale; and MCP servers vary in quality, some failing to run even when correctly configured. Reviewer-observed: (a) The 8.7%/27.2% prevalence figures reflect only the npx/uvx-installable, freely-registerable subset, so they are a lower bound of unknown tightness rather than a full-ecosystem census. (b) Ground truth for the LLM labeler is itself human annotation with only substantial (not near-perfect) agreement (Fleiss' Kappa 0.72), and capability-analysis precision is a modest 85.5%, so the risky-tool counts carry non-trivial labeling error. (c) RQ3 defines success as any 1-of-10 trial succeeding, which measures feasibility, not reliability, and inflates the headline '9 of 10 toolchains' relative to per-trial success rates (several chains succeed only 1-3/10). (d) All end-to-end tests run with auto-execution enabled and mostly with attack instructions presented in isolation; the paper concedes human-in-the-loop confirmation and mixed benign/malicious content could reduce real-world success and only argues (partly by citing others) that these are not reliable defenses. (e) The remote-command-execution and arbitrary-file-write variants are described conceptually as future work, not empirically measured.

datasets_used: Self-collected MCP census: 1,360 MCP servers exposing 12,230 tools, crawled from three sources
Awesome MCP Servers repo: 266 servers (19.6%), 2,713 tools (22.2%)
Servers restricted to those packaged for direct npx/uvx execution
RQ3 evaluation set: 10 hand-constructed attack toolchains from popular real tools (9 cross-server, 1 single-server), plus one fixed chain fetch->read_file->send_mail for the cross-model/client sweep
quantitative_results: 1,062 of 12,230 tools (8.7%) confirmed exploitable after dynamic verification (5,666 flagged by static capability analysis first)
370 of 1,360 servers (27.2%) expose at least one exploitable tool
Tool capability breakdown: 487 External Ingestion Tools, 406 Privacy Access Tools, 190 Network Access Tools; 2 tools cover all three capabilities
Server capability breakdown: 128 with EIT, 155 with PAT, 96 with NAT; 2 servers cover all three capabilities
LLM capability-analysis accuracy vs human ground truth: 85.5% precision, 98.0% recall (per-class EIT 86.5/99.2, PAT 85.4/95.8, NAT 81.1/99.6)
End-to-end: 9 of 10 toolchains succeeded (>=1 of 10 trials); Chains 1/3/4 succeeded 6-8/10; Chain 8 failed 0/10

Cross-model sweep: GPT-5 and Claude-4.5-Sonnet 0/5 (resisted all); DeepSeek-R1 and Gemini-2.5-Flash compromised in nearly every condition (frequently 5/5); Claude-3.5-Haiku and GPT-4.1 largely resistant (mostly 0-1/5)
82 of the risky servers (22%) have 100+ GitHub stars; several exceed 1,000 stars
Analysis cost: 255 hours automated (675 s/server, 75 s/tool); manual checks 71 h capability analysis + 143 h dynamic verification
Reported auto-approval adoption cited as motivation: ~20% of new users, >40% with experience
baselines_compared: No competing attack/detection system is benchmarked; this is a measurement + attack-feasibility study rather than a head-to-head comparison
Primary quantitative baseline is three-reviewer manual annotation (majority vote) treated as ground truth, against which the LLM labeler's precision/recall is measured
Comparative axes are internal: 7 LLMs (Cursor Auto, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, GPT-5, DeepSeek-R1, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Claude-3.5-Haiku, GPT-4.1), 5 MCP clients, and 3 prompt styles

Prior MCP attacks (Tool Poisoning / Rug Pull / Shadowing by Invariant Labs, malicious-server threats, single-server indirect injection) and defenses (MCP Guardian, MAESTRO/Zero-Trust, malicious-server detectors, MCPSecBench) are contrasted conceptually as cited related work, not empirically evaluated against MCP-UPD
